_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for St. Mary's County, MD. The oldest county records in Maryland — court records from 1637, only three years after the colony's founding. St. Mary's City was the original capital, and the county's records from the founding period are among the most important colonial documents in English America.
display_name: St. Mary's County, Maryland — Courthouse Atlas

records_housed: court records from the 19th century forward
some colonial records remain in Leonardtown; bulk of early records at Maryland State Archives

previous_courthouses.location: St. Mary's City — courts met in the State House and other public buildings at the colonial capital
previous_courthouses.fate: When the capital moved to Annapolis in 1694, St. Mary's City began its long decline. Some records moved with the provincial government to Annapolis; county-level records remained in St. Mary's County.

previous_courthouses.location: Leonardtown (county seat established circa 1710 after the capital moved to Annapolis). Early courthouses were frame structures.
previous_courthouses.fate: Successive frame courthouse buildings. No catastrophic fire or destruction of records. The county's isolation helped preserve records — no army marched through, no major fire swept the town.
Served for over a century. Records accumulated without catastrophic loss.

record_losses.cause: Incomplete record-keeping in the earliest colonial period; Ingle's Rebellion (1645) caused some disruption
records_destroyed: some early court proceedings from the 1640s are incomplete or fragmentary
Ingle's Rebellion (1644-1646) disrupted government operations and likely caused some record loss
records_survived: court proceedings from 1637 survive — among the oldest in English America
land records from the 1630s survive in the provincial patent books at the State Archives
the bulk of colonial records from the 1650s onward are remarkably complete
record_losses.description: St. Mary's County's record losses are remarkably minor for a county of its age. The earliest court records — from 1637 — survive, making them among the oldest continuous county court records in English America. Ingle's Rebellion (1644-1646) disrupted government operations temporarily, and some records from that period are fragmentary. But the bound volumes from the 1650s onward are substantially complete. The county's isolation — no major fire, no military destruction, no courthouse fire comparable to Prince George's County's 1882 disaster — preserved an extraordinary record.
record_losses.narrative_impact: St. Mary's County's records survived when other counties' did not — if {ancestor_name} was in the county, the paper trail stretches back to 1637, barely three years after the colony's founding.
record_losses.source: Maryland State Archives. Guide to Government Records. Annapolis, 2005.

deeds.completeness: Exceptional. Land records survive from the founding period, both in the county records and in the provincial patent books at the Maryland State Archives. St. Mary's County has one of the longest continuous land record series in English America.
deeds.gaps: Minor fragmentary gaps in the 1640s. Essentially complete from the 1650s onward.
deeds.repository: Maryland State Archives, Annapolis; St. Mary's County Circuit Court, Leonardtown

wills_estates.completeness: Very good. Early wills and inventories survive both in county records and in the Prerogative Court records at the State Archives. The colonial-era inventories are extraordinarily detailed.
wills_estates.gaps: Some fragmentation in the 1640s. The Prerogative Court copies provide backup for county records.
wills_estates.repository: Maryland State Archives (prerogative court and county records); St. Mary's County Register of Wills

marriages.completeness: Marriage licenses from 1777. Earlier marriages documented in church records (Catholic and Anglican) and court records (marriage banns published at court).
marriages.gaps: No marriage licenses before 1777. Church records and court proceedings provide substantial substitute documentation.
marriages.repository: Maryland State Archives; St. Mary's County Clerk of Court

court_minutes.completeness: Exceptional. Court proceedings from 1637 survive — among the oldest in English America. These early records document indentured servitude, land disputes, criminal cases, and the daily operations of the colony's first government.
court_minutes.gaps: Minor gaps during Ingle's Rebellion (1644-1646). Post-1650 records are substantially complete.
court_minutes.online_access: Maryland State Archives; Archives of Maryland Online (published colonial records)

vital_records.repository: Maryland Department of Health; Maryland State Archives

church_records.parish: St. Ignatius Church (Jesuit/Catholic, Chapel Point — served St. Mary's families); All Saints' Parish (Anglican/Episcopal); St. Andrew's Parish (Anglican); various Jesuit missions from the founding period
church_records.completeness: The Jesuit records from the Maryland mission — the founding religious institution of the colony — survive from 1634. These include sacramental registers, plantation records, and correspondence. Anglican parish vestry records from 1692. The combination of Catholic and Anglican records provides exceptional documentation for both religious communities.
church_records.repository: Georgetown University Library (Jesuit records); Maryland Province Archives; Maryland State Archives (Anglican records)
church_records.online_access: Published transcriptions; Georgetown digital collections; FamilySearch

substitute_sources.source_type: Archives of Maryland Online — published colonial records
substitute_sources.description: The Archives of Maryland Online (aomol.msa.maryland.gov) publishes transcriptions of colonial-era court proceedings, legislative records, and provincial records. St. Mary's County's earliest records are included in these published volumes, making them accessible to researchers worldwide.
substitute_sources.repository: Maryland State Archives — online at aomol.msa.maryland.gov
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Exceptional. The published colonial records include court proceedings that name individuals, document land transactions, resolve disputes over indentured servitude, and record criminal cases. For the founding period (1634-1700), these published records are the primary research tool.
substitute_sources.source: Archives of Maryland Online. Maryland State Archives, Annapolis.

substitute_sources.source_type: Historic St. Mary's City archaeological and documentary research
substitute_sources.description: Historic St. Mary's City, the outdoor museum and archaeological site at the location of the original colonial capital, has conducted decades of archaeological and documentary research that illuminates daily life in the founding period. Their research publications document specific individuals, structures, and events from the 1634-1694 capital period.
substitute_sources.repository: Historic St. Mary's City research library; St. Mary's College of Maryland
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Archaeological findings combined with documentary research can place specific individuals at specific locations in the capital — a rare combination of material and written evidence for the colonial period.
substitute_sources.source: Miller, Henry M. 'The Country's House Site: An Archaeological Study of a Seventeenth-Century Domestic Landscape.' In Historical Archaeology of the Chesapeake, ed. Paul A. Shackel and Barbara J. Little. Washington, DC: Smithsonian Institution Press, 1994.

narrative_hooks: St. Mary's County's records begin in 1637 — three years after the colony's founding, and among the oldest continuous English records in America. If {ancestor_name} was there early enough, the documentation stretches back nearly four centuries.

The Jesuit records from the founding period document Catholic families, enslaved workers, and the daily operations of the missions — if {ancestor_name} was Catholic in St. Mary's County, the Jesuit sacramental registers may hold baptisms and marriages that the county court never recorded.

St. Mary's County never lost its records to fire or war — the county's isolation preserved an extraordinary paper trail that other Western Shore counties lost to courthouse fires and military occupation.

The Archives of Maryland Online publishes the colonial court records of St. Mary's County in searchable transcription — {ancestor_name}'s court appearance in the 1650s or 1660s may be readable on a screen three and a half centuries later.
source_references: Maryland State Archives. Guide to Government Records. Annapolis, 2005.
Archives of Maryland Online. Maryland State Archives, Annapolis.
Miller, Henry M. 'The Country's House Site.' In Historical Archaeology of the Chesapeake, ed. Shackel and Little. Smithsonian Institution Press, 1994.
Carr, Lois Green, Russell R. Menard, and Lorena S. Walsh. Robert Cole's World. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1991.
